narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 45% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for admission. This prediction is based on a careful look at key aspects of your academic and background profile, including your bottom-decile ranking in Year 1 school grades, your second-decile ranking in Year 3, your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, LSAT score of 35, upper-middle family income bracket, status as a male White student planning part-time work. Compared to typical students—who average around the middle of their class in both early and later years, a 3.21 undergrad GPA, and a 36.7 LSAT—your standings in school deciles and test scores are notably lower, which stood out to the model. The strongest influence on this prediction came from your Year 3 decile ranking, as it significantly pushed the model toward expecting a bar failure. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile, which had a similar pulling effect in the same direction. Your LSAT score followed closely, contributing further to the concern about passing the bar. Other factors like your family income, undergrad GPA, racial background, part-time work plans, and gender also nudged the prediction toward failure, though with progressively less weight in that order. None of these elements pulled strongly in favor of success for you, unlike many admitted students whose profiles balance out with stronger academics. While demographics and family background are harder to change, you can take concrete steps to boost your odds moving forward, such as retaking the LSAT to aim above the average or building a stronger academic record in any further studies. Switching to full-time enrollment could also help, as most successful bar takers go that route. Focusing on these adjustable areas—especially grades and test scores—has turned things around for many students in similar spots. We encourage you to use this feedback as a roadmap and reapply once you've strengthened those key predictors. Keep pushing; your upper-middle background gives you solid support to make these improvements.
